_meta.note: BuzzFeed quiz tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: On the Origin of Species Dopamine Master Quiz · Charles Darwin
html_001: Charles Darwin · 1859 · A classic of evolution
html_002: Where did the diversity of life come from?
html_003: On the Origin of Species Master Quiz
html_004: “There is grandeur in this view of life. From so simple a beginning
html_005: endless forms most beautiful have been, and are being, evolved.”
html_006: — Charles Darwin, On the Origin of Species
html_007: The history of life through natural selection — its key ideas, evidence, and debates —
html_009:
. You learn it here.
        It starts easy and gets harder,
html_010: and wrong questions come back until you get them right.
html_027: That’s the end of the free preview
html_040: On the Origin of Species at a glance
html_043: and you’ll never forget it. Every starting point is
html_044: variation that differs from individual to individual
html_048: The raw material of natural selection
html_051: More are born than can survive
html_054: Those that fit the environment leave more offspring
html_056: 🌿 Passed on by heredity · piling up over time
html_057: Small changes build up and traits diverge
html_059: 🌳 A tree of life branching from a common ancestor
html_060: “From a simple beginning to endless forms most beautiful”
html_062: Table of contents at a glance
html_064: 3 cases from the book worth pondering
html_066: Focused review of what you missed
html_068: lets you explore Darwin and evolution in more depth.
html_074: A repeat master quiz that drills a book’s core with dopamine
html_075: This app is secondary learning content that reworks the book’s core ideas for study purposes. For the full reading experience, meet the original book itself.
html_076: On the Origin of Species · Book info

js_004: Who was the English naturalist who published On the Origin of Species in 1859?
js_009: On the Origin of Species is a book the English naturalist Charles Darwin published in 1859. It was the first to set out systematically that living things evolve over long stretches of time through natural selection, and it completely changed the course of biology.
js_011: In which year did On the Origin of Species first appear?
js_016: The first edition of On the Origin of Species came out in Britain in November 1859. Its 1,500 copies sold out on the first day, and Darwin kept revising the text right up to the sixth edition (1872).
js_018: What did Darwin propose as the most important driving force behind evolution?
js_023: Darwin saw the main force of evolution as natural selection. Individuals with even slightly advantageous traits survive and leave more offspring, and as those traits stack up over generations, living things slowly change.
js_025: What was the name of the ship on which Darwin sailed for about five years, gathering the crucial clues for his theory of evolution?
js_029: At twenty-two, Darwin joined the naval survey ship HMS Beagle as a naturalist and spent about five years (1831–1836) roaming South America and many islands. The plant, animal, and fossil observations he gathered became the bedrock of his later theory of evolution.
js_031: Which volcanic archipelago near the equator, where Darwin observed slightly different creatures on each island, became the birthplace of his theory?
js_036: On the equatorial Galápagos Islands, the environment differs slightly from island to island, and creatures descended from the same stock were living in different forms. From this difference Darwin caught the thread of an idea: that species can change.
js_038: Which small bird, whose beak shape differs from island to island in the Galápagos, became a symbol of natural selection?
js_043: These are the famous Darwin’s finches. Food differs by island, and their beaks diverged into all shapes — from thick ones for cracking seeds to slender ones for catching insects. They’re the classic case of adaptive radiation: many species from one ancestor.
js_045: Which practice — breeding livestock and crops chosen for the traits people want — did Darwin use as an analogy to explain natural selection?
js_047: Nature culling individuals on its own
js_048: Use and disuse developing an organ
js_049: A special creation shaping species from scratch
js_050: Darwin first shows artificial selection: choosing the traits people want — in pigeons, dogs, crops — and letting them pass down. What if nature did this selecting instead of us? That question is the key to understanding natural selection.
js_052: Because far more individuals are born than can survive, what goes on ceaselessly among living things?
js_054: Perfect coexistence, all thriving side by side
js_055: Population control preset by a god
js_056: A peace where everyone thrives equally
js_057: Darwin took a hint from Malthus’s essay on population. Since creatures are always born in greater numbers than can survive, a struggle for existence rages endlessly over food, space, and mates.
js_059: What is Darwin’s central claim — that all life on Earth ultimately branched from a single ancestor?
js_062: Living things never change at all
js_063: All species are unrelated to each other
js_064: Darwin held that, however different they look, all living things branched over vast time from one common ancestor. It means humans and fish, trees and moulds are all one enormous family.
js_066: What single visual metaphor did Darwin use to depict how creatures branch and how they’re related?
js_070: Darwin drew evolution as a tree of life. Branches split from one trunk (the common ancestor); the surviving branch tips are today’s species, the broken-off branches the extinct ones. The one and only illustration in On the Origin of Species is exactly this tree.
js_071: Wikipedia: On the Origin of Species
js_079: EBS Documentary: The Age of God and Darwin
js_080: Charles Darwin (1809–1882) was an English naturalist. After chewing over the observations from his Beagle voyage for more than twenty years, he published On the Origin of Species, and went on to tackle human evolution in The Descent of Man.
js_081: After the 1859 first edition, Darwin revised the book edition after edition, answering his critics. In the 6th edition (1872) he even adopted Spencer’s phrase ‘survival of the fittest’ into the text.
js_082: The heart of natural selection is that ‘variations favourable to the environment survive more and carry on down the generations’. There is no purpose or plan — the side that happens to fit simply remains.
js_083: The Beagle voyage (1831–1836) was the turning point of Darwin’s life. South American fossils, Andean strata, and Galápagos creatures all became seeds of the idea that ‘species change’.
js_084: The Galápagos, isolated island by island, became a natural laboratory of evolution. Darwin missed its meaning at first, growing sure of species change only later, as he sorted his specimens back home.
js_085: Darwin’s finches split from one ancestor and came to have different beaks island by island. They’re a visible example of adaptive radiation and natural selection — traits diverging to match the food.
js_086: Artificial selection is the bridge to understanding natural selection. Just as people breed varieties by choosing the traits they want, in nature the ‘environment’ plays the selector — that was Darwin’s insight.
js_087: The struggle for existence isn’t always a bloody fight. Enduring a drought, or finding food better, is competition too. The core is the plain fact that ‘more are born than can survive’.
js_088: The idea of common ancestry ties all of life into one. Humans, fish, and trees split from the same root long ago — and molecular biology strongly backs this up today.
js_089: The one and only illustration in On the Origin of Species is this tree of life. Branches split from the trunk, and most break off partway — showing that extinction is the rule, not the exception.
js_095: On the Origin of Species — evolution complete
js_103: Individuals with variations that fit their environment survive and leave more offspring. It’s the beating heart of Darwin’s theory.
js_105: Variation differing from individual to individual is the raw material; useful variations pass down by heredity and pile up across generations.
js_106: More are born than can survive, so there’s constant competition over food, space, and mates.
js_107: Common ancestry & the tree of life
js_108: Every living thing is a branch on one tree of life that split from a single ancestor.
js_110: Finches whose beaks split to match each island’s food show adaptation — traits changing to fit the environment — right before your eyes.
js_112: The same ancestor adapts to different environments, diverges, and finally splits into many species.
js_114: Selection for reproduction, not survival. Like the peacock’s showy tail, traits that win mates evolve.
js_116: Tiny variations pile up over vast geological time into big change. Evolution isn’t a leap — it’s accumulation.
js_118: Variation under domestication and in nature
js_119: People bred pigeons, dogs, and crops to their liking and created astonishing varieties.
js_120: From this ‘artificial selection’, Darwin reasons that nature must have a similar selection.
js_121: Wild creatures, too, carry slightly different ‘variations’ from individual to individual — the raw material of natural selection.
js_122: No variation, nothing to select — variation is where evolution begins.
js_124: The struggle for existence and natural selection
js_125: Taking a hint from Malthus, he notes that creatures are born in greater numbers than can survive.
js_126: So a constant ‘struggle for existence’ breaks out over food, space, and mates.
js_127: In this struggle, variations that fit the environment survive — that is natural selection.
js_128: Useful variations accumulate through heredity, and creatures change slowly but surely.
js_131: Darwin didn’t know exactly why or how variation arises.
js_132: Even so, he builds his theory on the plain fact that variation exists and is inherited.
js_133: This gap in heredity would later be filled by Mendel’s genetics.
js_136: Could an organ as intricate as the eye arise by natural selection? He raises his own weak point.
js_137: From light-sensing cells to the complete eye, he shows the intermediate stages really exist.
js_138: Even complex behaviour like the instinct to build a hive, he explains, was shaped by natural selection.
js_139: Here Darwin’s honesty shines — he faces the difficulties head-on instead of hiding them.
js_142: The scarcity of intermediate fossils linking species was a serious weakness.
js_143: Darwin answers that the fossil record itself is incomplete — time was on his side.
js_144: How creatures are spread across islands and continents fits neatly with ‘they spread from a common ancestor’.
js_145: Isolated islands like the Galápagos show the traces of evolution most clearly.
js_147: Classification, form, embryology — and the conclusion
js_148: Similar bone structures and alike embryonic development all converge as evidence of common ancestry.
js_149: Scattered facts are threaded together into one ‘tree of life’.
js_150: Darwin closes the book with his famous line: ‘There is grandeur in this view of life’.
js_151: From the simplest beginnings, endless forms most beautiful have evolved — and still do.
js_152: The peppered moth in factory soot
js_153: When the Industrial Revolution blackened the trees, pale moths stood out to birds and were eaten, while dark moths survived and multiplied. As the air cleared, pale moths took over again.
js_154: Natural selection unfolding in just a few generations, right before our eyes — a textbook case that evolution is still at work.
js_155: The drought that changed a finch’s beak
js_156: When drought hit the Galápagos and only hard seeds remained, finches with thicker beaks survived in greater numbers. Even a single drought visibly enlarged the average beak.
js_157: A case measured in the actual wild, showing that natural selection is not always slow.
js_159: Use an antibiotic and the vulnerable bacteria die, while those that happen to be resistant survive and spread. That’s why resistant strains keep rising and are a major problem today.
js_160: A reminder that natural selection isn’t some abstract old tale but a present reality threatening our health.
js_161: If humans are a product of the same evolution as every other creature, what happens to the belief that we’re special?
js_162: Common ancestry places humans inside nature, not outside it. If we can still be special, ask yourself where that specialness comes from.
js_163: If natural selection has no predetermined purpose, is there no meaning in life’s diversity and beauty?
js_164: That astonishing order arose from a purposeless process may itself be the wonder. Is meaning found, or is it given?
js_165: If evolution isn’t past but ongoing, what are we changing toward?
js_166: Like resistant bacteria or moths, humans are still under selective pressure. Only now the direction is set not just by nature but by the environment we’ve built.
js_168: You read On the Origin of Species perfectly
js_169: 🔬 A naturalist who grasped natural selection
js_170: You’ve got almost all the essentials
js_171: 🐦 An explorer just landing on the Galápagos
